metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `acli auth login` (or equivalent non-interactive credential-file setup) prerequisite to the harness setup section and Isolation/credentials AC, specifying how the service-account token populates the ACLI config file in the CI environment (e.g., `acli auth login --server $JIRA_URL --user $JIRA_USER --password $JIRA_API_TOKEN --no-input` or a pre-baked credential file mounted as a secret), since `_build_env()` does not inject credentials into the ACLI subprocess.
findings.finding: The ACLI subprocess path does NOT read credentials from environment variables or from AcliClient attributes at call time. `_build_env()` (acli_subprocess.py:182-184) is `os.environ.copy()` only — no credential injection — and the `_run()` docstring (acli.py:256-259) explicitly states 'ACLI Go reads auth from its config file (set by `acli auth login`). Credentials stored on self are NOT injected into the subprocess environment.' The plan's auth precondition ('service account token via a secret') covers the REST mixin path but silently omits the `acli auth login` prerequisite for ALL ACLI subprocess operations (including `delete_issue`, create, update, search). A harness that injects only `JIRA_API_TOKEN` into the environment will see every ACLI subprocess call fail with a 401/auth error.
findings.location: Isolation, credentials section; AC-4 (delete-permission probe); Harness section (bilateral teardown)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Isolation/credentials section, add: 'Harness setup step 0: run `acli auth login --server $JIRA_URL --user $JIRA_USER --password $JIRA_API_TOKEN --no-input` (or mount a pre-baked ACLI credential file) before any ACLI subprocess call. The ACLI Go binary reads auth from its config file only (`acli.py:256-259`); `JIRA_API_TOKEN` in the environment is consumed by the REST mixin path only, not by ACLI subprocess calls.' Update AC-4 to read: 'Delete-permission probe passes after `acli auth login` completes; ...'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the echo-suppression setup instruction: replace 'baseline-seeded via the `ProvenanceLedger`' with a description of the two distinct mechanisms — (1) run one real reconciler pass (which calls `binding_store.seed_baselines_from_snapshot()` for scalar field idempotency) AND (2) the ProvenanceLedger echo-suppression for comment/label collection elements (seeded automatically by `compute_mutations` during that same first pass) — so the harness pre-seeds both correctly.
findings.finding: The echo-suppression signal setup instruction says 'baseline-seeded via the ProvenanceLedger (defined in conflict_resolver.py)' but the ProvenanceLedger is the element-level echo-suppression mechanism for comments/labels, not the field-level baseline used for idempotency; the harness pre-seeding description conflates two distinct mechanisms, which could cause an implementer to instrument only the ProvenanceLedger path and miss the binding_store baseline seed.
findings.location: Signal definitions, 'Echo suppression' bullet: 'field/status writes are baseline-seeded via the `ProvenanceLedger` (defined in `conflict_resolver.py`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'field/status writes are baseline-seeded via the `ProvenanceLedger` (defined in `conflict_resolver.py`)' with: 'field/status writes are baseline-seeded by running one full reconciler pass (which calls `binding_store.seed_baselines_from_snapshot()`); collection-element echo suppression uses the `ProvenanceLedger` populated by `compute_mutations` during that same pass — both are seeded by the single prior-pass run, not by direct ledger manipulation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the ProvenanceLedger pre-seeding protocol in the echo-suppression signal definition: which `element_key` format to use (field name vs. field:value), which `side` ('local' or 'jira') for outbound vs. inbound writes, and at what harness step to call `.record()` before the idempotency pass.
findings.finding: The echo-suppression signal definition says 'field/status writes are baseline-seeded via the `ProvenanceLedger` (defined in `conflict_resolver.py`) — verify `mutation_count==0` on the next pass over just-written data (harness pre-seeds the baseline as a real pass would)' but never defines the protocol for how the harness pre-seeds the ProvenanceLedger. The `ProvenanceLedger.record()` API requires: (a) a specific `element_key` format (`"<field_name>:<element_value>"` for collections or `<field_name>` for scalars), (b) `side` must be 'local' or 'jira' (raises ValueError if None), and (c) a `value` to hash. Without specifying which element_keys, which side, and at what point in the harness lifecycle to call `.record()`, an executor cannot replicate 'as a real pass would'.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, 'Echo suppression' bullet; conflict_resolver.py:227-263

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC[2]'s blast-radius assertion to replace the unguarded `census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` with `check_blast_radius(...).allowed == False` (or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)['breaker']['allowed'] == False`) so the AC and Round-6 corrections are consistent.
findings.finding: The AC[2] verify step still contains the unguarded `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` expression, which directly contradicts the Round-6 corrections section that explicitly says 'do NOT index [breaker] unguarded' and instructs using `check_blast_radius(...)` or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)` instead.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC[2] vs. Round-6 corrections ('BREAKER ASSERTION')
findings.suggested_fix: AC[2]: change 'blast-radius via `classify.census()[\'breaker\'][\'allowed\']==False`' to 'blast-radius via `check_blast_radius(acting_count, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction=...).allowed == False` (classify.py)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC bullet 2 to replace the unguarded `census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` with the Round-6-correct form: either `check_blast_radius(...).allowed == False` or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)['breaker']['allowed'] == False` with explicit kwargs, so the AC and Round-6 correction are consistent.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria still contain the unguarded `census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` assertion that Round-6 explicitly warns will raise a KeyError when called without the required `max_acting_fraction` and `total_bindings` kwargs on a non-acting pass — the AC text was not updated to match the Round-6 correction, leaving a direct contradiction that an implementer following the AC literally will produce a failing harness.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 2 vs. Round-6 corrections ('BREAKER ASSERTION')
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC bullet, change: `blast-radius via \`classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False\`` to `blast-radius via \`classify.check_blast_radius(acting_count, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction).allowed == False\`` (or the explicit-kwargs census form); remove or merge the Round-6 note into the AC so there is one authoritative statement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC2's blast-radius assertion to use `check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction).allowed == False` or `census(decisions, max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)["breaker"]["allowed"] == False` (with both kwargs), consistent with the Round-6 correction already stated in this plan.
findings.finding: AC2 still contains the unguarded `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` expression that Round-6 corrections (same document, section 'Round-6 corrections') explicitly states will raise KeyError when called without `max_acting_fraction` and `total_bindings` kwargs. The codebase at classify.py:368 confirms: `if max_acting_fraction is not None and total_bindings is not None:` — without both kwargs the 'breaker' key is absent from the returned dict. Round-6 prescribes using `check_blast_radius(...).allowed` or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)` explicitly, but AC2 was not updated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 2 ('blast-radius via `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False`') vs. Round-6 corrections section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` in AC2 with `classify.check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.09).allowed == False`, which matches classify.py:305-336 and the Round-6 prescription.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC line 2's blast-radius assertion to match Round-6: replace `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` with `classify.check_blast_radius(...).allowed == False` (or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)['breaker']['allowed']` with explicit kwargs) to eliminate the KeyError path.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria (AC line 2) still contains the unguarded `census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` expression. The Round-6 corrections section in the same plan explicitly states this will KeyError unless `max_acting_fraction` AND `total_bindings` are both passed, and directs the executor to use `check_blast_radius(...)` returning a `BreakerVerdict` with `.allowed == False` instead — but the AC text was never updated to reflect this. The blast-radius signal definition body also retains the original unguarded form. An executor reading the AC directly will implement a KeyError-raising assertion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 2 and Signal definitions / Blast-radius breaker paragraph; vs. Round-6 corrections section
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC, change `blast-radius via \`classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False\`` to `blast-radius via \`classify.check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction).allowed == False\` (classify.py:305)`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the contradictory breaker assertion: update BOTH the AC bullet and the Signal definitions NOTE to read the single canonical form — either `check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction).allowed == False` or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=..., decisions=...)['breaker']['allowed'] == False` — and remove the unguarded `census()['breaker']` pattern from both locations.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria (binding contract for implementers) retains the unguarded `census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` assertion that the Round-6 corrections explicitly say will KeyError — the two sections contradict each other and the AC takes precedence at implementation time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet: 'blast-radius via `classify.census()["breaker"]["allowed"]==False`'; also Signal definitions 'Assert via the breaker API in `classify.py`: `census()["breaker"]["allowed"] == False`'; Round-6 corrections paragraph 'BREAKER ASSERTION'
findings.suggested_fix: In both the AC bullet and the Signal definitions NOTE, replace the unguarded form with: `check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.09).allowed == False` (using classify.check_blast_radius directly, which is already the canonical path used in binding_walk.py:238 and is always callable without a 'breaker' key guard).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update AC line 2 to replace `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` with either `check_blast_radius(...).allowed == False` or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)['breaker']['allowed']`, matching the Round-6 correction already stated in this plan.
findings.finding: AC line 2 retains the unguarded `census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` expression that Round-6 corrections (in the same plan document) explicitly state will raise a KeyError when called without `max_acting_fraction` and `total_bindings` kwargs. The Round-6 section prescribes using `check_blast_radius(...)` or calling `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)` explicitly — but the AC body was never updated. An executor following AC line 2 literally writes a broken assertion, while the corrections section co-located in the same plan says the opposite.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 2; Round-6 corrections section ('BREAKER ASSERTION'); classify.py:368
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the AC line 2 blast-radius clause with: `blast-radius via `check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.09).allowed == False` on the mass-change scenario` — this calls the function directly without the conditional key lookup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a discovery rule to the C3 signal definition specifying how the harness identifies the C3 BRIDGE_ALERT at validation time (e.g. by tag/kind value from C3's implementation, or by checking `data['field']` matches the dropped field name), mirroring the C1 'reference C1's implemented signal at validation time' pattern.
findings.finding: The C3 Signal definition says 'assert the post-C3 deduped BRIDGE_ALERT C3 (nonglacial-tense-vulture) ADDS fires on a dropped outbound field' but never defines the assertion vocabulary: which BRIDGE_ALERT `data` tag or `kind` value identifies this alert, what the dedup key is (per-ticket, per-field, or per-(ticket,field)), or what file-path glob pattern to use. The C3 ticket (nonglacial-tense-vulture) AC only says 'emit a deduped alert' and 'deduped per (ticket, field)' — the alert's internal tag/kind field is unspecified in both the ticket and the plan. An executor cannot write a concrete assertion without knowing the observable field values that distinguish a C3 field-drop alert from other BRIDGE_ALERT types.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, C3 bullet; nonglacial-tense-vulture ticket AC

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the C4 signal definition body with Round-6: remove `and/or \`_call_with_retry\` (dispatch_one.py)` from the C4 signal text so it names only `_run_acli` as the single patch target, matching the Round-6 correction.
findings.finding: `_call_with_backoff` in `acli_subprocess.py:236` is dead code with zero production call sites in `src/` — it is only exercised by one test (`test_error_taxonomy.py`). The C4 signal definition in the plan still mentions it by name ('NOT the dead `_call_with_backoff`'), acknowledging it is dead, but the Round-6 correction's instruction to patch 'the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop' is inconsistent with the C4 signal body's separate mention of `_call_with_retry (dispatch_one.py)` as a co-target. `_run_acli` and `_call_with_retry` are architecturally disjoint: the former is a subprocess-level loop retrying on `CalledProcessError`; the latter wraps Python callables retrying on `urllib` errors. The plan names both as a C4 patch target in the signal definition body despite Round-6 claiming to have resolved this by naming only `_run_acli`.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C4 section; Round-6 corrections section
findings.suggested_fix: In the C4 signal definition, change '...the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop and/or `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py)...' to '...the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop (acli_subprocess.py:337)...' only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove 'and/or `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py)' from the C4 signal definition body so it names only `_run_acli` (acli_subprocess.py), consistent with the Round-6 correction already stated in this plan.
findings.finding: The C4 Signal definition names two structurally incompatible patch targets in the same sentence: 'monkey-patch the LIVE retry path C4 (lambswool-shaded-eider) implements — the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop and/or `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py)'. These are architecturally separate retry mechanisms operating on different exception domains (subprocess `CalledProcessError` vs. REST `JiraAPIError`). Round-6 corrections say 'patch the SINGLE live path C4 lands (per lambswool-shaded-eider) — the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop ... Do not name two incompatible targets.' — but the original Signal definition text was not updated, leaving the 'and/or `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py)' clause intact.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, C4 bullet; Round-6 corrections section ('C4 TARGET'); acli_subprocess.py:337; dispatch_one.py:100
findings.suggested_fix: Edit the C4 signal line to read: 'monkey-patch the LIVE retry path C4 (lambswool-shaded-eider) implements — the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop in `acli_subprocess.py`' and remove the `_call_with_retry` reference entirely from the signal definition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default assertion for C1 (e.g. 'assert conflict record has `status == conflict` and `local_id == feline-wandering-dassierat`') or add an AC that the harness reads C1's manifest at setup and fails loud if the field is absent — do not leave the field discovery to executor judgment at validation time.
findings.finding: C1 assertion field is explicitly deferred to runtime ('do NOT hard-code an unverified field; reference C1's implemented signal at validation time'), meaning an executor cannot write the C1 assertion without stopping to investigate what field/value C1 actually lands.
findings.location: Signal definitions / C1 bullet; AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a setup step: `c1_field = load_signal_manifest('feline-wandering-dassierat')['conflict_field']; assert c1_field is not None, 'C1 manifest missing — abort'` and assert `jq -e --arg f "$c1_field" '.[$f] != null' c1_record.json`.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pick one harness pattern (recommend: pytest autouse fixture with `yield`+`finally` for Python-native assertion integration) and state it as the decision; remove the 'if suitable / else' branching.
findings.finding: The harness implementation is left to executor judgment via 'Build on the existing e2e_validation_probe.sh if suitable; else a pytest autouse fixture ... or bash trap cleanup EXIT' — three options with no decision criterion, making this a 'choose an appropriate X' placeholder.
findings.location: Harness section, first bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the Harness teardown-failure fallback to normatively direct the executor to call `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (or its re-export via `batch_dispatch`) for cleanup retries rather than specifying a new bespoke 3x loop — the existing function already provides the required semantics at 15+ production call-sites.
findings.finding: The Harness section introduces a new bespoke '3x exponential backoff' retry loop for teardown cleanup calls without anchoring it to `dispatch_one._call_with_retry`, which already provides identical semantics (delays=[1,2,4], 429/5xx/TimeoutError retryable, max_retries=3) and is used at 15+ production call-sites. The Round-6 correction says 'reuse an existing backoff helper... rather than a bespoke 3x loop, where practical', but this remains advisory in a corrections appendix rather than a normative directive in the Harness body — the plan body and AC still phrase teardown retry as undifferentiated new infrastructure, leaving an NIH reimplementation path open to the executor.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback: each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff'; Round-6 corrections — 'TEARDOWN RETRY'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff' in the Harness body with: 'each cleanup call is wrapped with `_call_with_retry` (from `dispatch_one`/`batch_dispatch`, already imported by `apply_outbound`) — the existing 3-retry exponential-backoff helper; on `RetryExhaustedError` after exhaustion, append the id to `leaked-artifacts.log`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the teardown retry prior-art gap: either (a) document why neither `_call_with_retry` nor `_call_with_backoff` is suitable for the `delete_issue` / `_run_acli` teardown path and specify the bespoke loop parameters, or (b) name a concrete reuse path (e.g., a thin wrapper around `_run_acli`'s own retry loop) and confirm it handles `CalledProcessError` from the ACLI subprocess.
findings.finding: The teardown-failure fallback specifies a bespoke '3x exp-backoff retry loop' for cleanup calls without acknowledging that two exponential-backoff implementations already exist in the codebase: `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py:100, handles JiraAPIError/429/5xx) and `_call_with_backoff` (acli_subprocess.py:236, handles urllib HTTPError/429 with Retry-After). The Round-6 correction says 'reuse an existing backoff helper … where practical' but doesn't resolve which one applies to teardown (which calls `client.delete_issue` via `_run_acli`, a subprocess path — incompatible with `_call_with_retry`'s JiraAPIError domain). The plan thus neither resolves the reinvention nor documents why neither existing helper fits.
findings.location: Harness section — 'Teardown-failure fallback' + Round-6 corrections 'TEARDOWN RETRY'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the unguarded `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` assertion from the Signal definitions section and make `check_blast_radius(...).allowed == False` (or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...) ['breaker']['allowed'] == False`) the single canonical proving command everywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: Two contradictory blast-radius assertion patterns coexist: Signal definitions says `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` (unguarded index), while Round-6 corrections says 'do NOT index [breaker] unguarded' and recommends `check_blast_radius(...)` returning `BreakerVerdict.allowed`. An executor cannot determine which pattern is canonical.
findings.location: Signal definitions / Blast-radius breaker bullet; Round-6 corrections / BREAKER ASSERTION bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In Signal definitions replace: 'Assert via the breaker API in classify.py: census()["breaker"]["allowed"] == False' with 'Assert `check_blast_radius(acting=N, total=M, max_acting_fraction=0.09).allowed == False` (classify.py) — do not index census()["breaker"] unguarded.' Delete the Round-6 BREAKER ASSERTION bullet as resolved.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the 'and/or `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py)' clause from the C4 Signal definition so it unambiguously targets only `_run_acli`, consistent with the Round-6 correction.
findings.finding: The C4 Signal definition still uses 'and/or' wording ('the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop and/or `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py)'), while the Round-6 corrections section explicitly says 'patch the SINGLE live path C4 lands — the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop' and 'Do not name two incompatible targets.' These are contradictory instructions in two separate sections.
findings.location: Signal definitions (C4) vs. Round-6 corrections ('C4 TARGET')
findings.suggested_fix: C4 signal definition: replace 'the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop and/or `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py)' with 'the `_run_acli` subprocess retry loop (the single live path C4 lands)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Commit to ONE harness pattern and state why: either (a) extend e2e_validation_probe.sh by replacing `set -euo pipefail` exit-on-error with explicit error capture + `trap cleanup EXIT`, OR (b) use a pytest autouse session-scoped fixture with `yield`+`finally` — and include a one-sentence positive rationale (e.g. 'pytest chosen because the partial-setup tracking state is naturally a Python object, simplifying the teardown-under-failure test').
findings.finding: The Harness section offers two mutually exclusive implementation patterns ('build on e2e_validation_probe.sh if suitable; else a pytest autouse fixture … or bash trap cleanup EXIT') without committing to one or providing a rationale for the choice — the existing script lacks trap cleanup EXIT and uses set -euo pipefail, making 'if suitable' load-bearing but unanswered.
findings.location: Harness section: 'Build on the existing `e2e_validation_probe.sh` if suitable; else a pytest autouse fixture `yield`+`finally` (or bash `trap cleanup EXIT`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-run unique prefix/suffix (e.g., run UUID) to all synthetic `rebar-id` labels and Jira issue summaries, so concurrent runs operate on disjoint namespaces within the shared TEST project.
findings.finding: The plan specifies a dedicated Jira TEST project for isolation but does not address concurrent execution safety — two simultaneous CI runs (e.g., a retry and a new push) against the same TEST project could create overlapping synthetic bindings with the same `rebar-id` labels, causing false-positive or false-negative assertions on label uniqueness, blast-radius counts, and grace-period retirement.
findings.location: Isolation, credentials section — 'Dedicated Jira TEST project'
findings.suggested_fix: Generate a `RUN_ID=$(uuidgen)` at harness start; prefix all synthetic labels as `rebar-id:${RUN_ID}-<local_id>` and filter all assertions to that prefix. Teardown deletes only issues matching that prefix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete report path (e.g. `reports/rebar-e2e-results.xml`) and CI artifact upload step (e.g. `actions/upload-artifact` or equivalent) so the output location is unambiguous and verifiable.
findings.finding: The JSON/JUnit report 'named path' is unnamed — the AC says 'writes a JSON/JUnit report to a named path attached to the ticket' but no path or filename is specified, so the output location cannot be verified.
findings.location: AC line 2: 'harness exits 0 all-green and writes a JSON/JUnit report to a named path attached to the ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 2: 'writes a JUnit report to `reports/rebar-e2e-results.xml`; CI uploads it as artifact name `rebar-e2e-report`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete output path (or a configurable env-var with a documented default, e.g. `REBAR_E2E_REPORT_PATH=test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml`) for the JSON/JUnit report so CI artifact upload rules can be configured deterministically.
findings.finding: The JSON/JUnit report is described as written to 'a named path attached to the ticket,' but no path is ever named anywhere in the plan. CI pipeline configuration (artifact upload rules, test-result parsing) requires a known, stable path at plan time.
findings.location: AC line: 'harness exits 0 all-green and writes a JSON/JUnit report to a named path attached to the ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC or Isolation section: 'Report written to `$REBAR_E2E_REPORT_PATH` (default: `test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml`); CI uploads `test-results/` as an artifact.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the hardcoded blast-radius test parameters (fraction=0.09, count>=11) with values derived from or cross-referenced against the authoritative constant/config in classify.py (e.g. import the threshold symbol or read it from the same config source), so the test stays coupled to production behavior under maintenance.
findings.finding: The blast-radius scenario hardcodes test parameters (fraction 0.09, >=11 synthetic bindings) that mirror the production threshold in classify.py, but does not derive them from the same source. If the production max_acting_fraction threshold changes in classify.py, the test silently exercises a mismatched threshold and may no longer validate the correct trip/no-trip boundary.
findings.location: Signal definitions — 'Blast-radius breaker' paragraph; AC line 'blast-radius via classify.census()[\'breaker\'][\'allowed\']==False'
findings.suggested_fix: Import the production threshold constant from classify.py (e.g. `from classify import MAX_ACTING_FRACTION`) and compute the synthetic binding count as `math.ceil(1 / MAX_ACTING_FRACTION) + 1` rather than asserting 0.09/11 as literals.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit backoff/retry (reusing the app-layer helper already referenced for teardown) to the synthetic-issue creation loop in the harness setup, and ensure the teardown trap is registered before the FIRST create call.
findings.finding: The setup phase creates >=11 synthetic Jira issues (required for the blast-radius scenario) with no specified rate-limit handling. A 429 during bulk synthetic-issue creation could leave the harness in a partially-initialized state with some artifacts created and tracked but setup incomplete, yet teardown may not be triggered if the error propagates as a setup exception before the trap/fixture is active.
findings.location: Signal definitions — 'Blast-radius breaker: with >=11 synthetic bindings'; Harness section
findings.suggested_fix: Register the cleanup trap/fixture at process start (before any Jira calls), then wrap the creation loop with the same retry helper used for teardown (e.g., dispatch_one's backoff). This ensures both 429 resilience and teardown coverage even if creation partially fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the concrete secret-injection mechanism for the Jira service-account token (e.g., GitHub Actions encrypted secret surfaced as a masked env var `${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}`); require the harness to consume it as an environment variable only (never as a CLI argument or written to disk), and confirm the CI step does NOT run with `set -x` or equivalent verbose output while the credential is in scope.
findings.finding: The plan declares the Jira service-account token must be 'never logged/committed' and 'injected via secret', but never names the concrete secrets-injection mechanism (e.g., GitHub Actions encrypted secret, environment variable from a vault, CI secret binding). This leaves the boundary-crossing credential path with an undeclared access posture: a reader cannot determine whether the token flows through a safe channel (masked env var in CI) or a leaky one (hardcoded in a script, echoed by `set -x`, or passed as a CLI argument substring).
findings.location: Isolation, credentials section; AC line: 'credential scoped to the test project + injected via secret'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Isolation, credentials' section, add: 'Token injected via a GitHub Actions encrypted repository secret (`${{ secrets.JIRA_API_TOKEN }}`), surfaced to the harness as an environment variable only. The harness step must not enable `set -x` or equivalent shell tracing while the variable is in scope. The token must never appear in subprocess argument lists (pass via env, not CLI arg).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the ProvenanceLedger for the test run uses an isolated namespace or temp file (created fresh each run and deleted in teardown), and that the harness asserts the ledger is empty before pre-seeding to detect stale state.
findings.finding: The ProvenanceLedger (used for echo-suppression / idempotency assertions) is described as pre-seeded by the harness 'as a real pass would', but the plan does not specify whether the ledger is isolated per test run (e.g., a temp file/DB namespace), who is responsible for cleaning it up post-run, or what happens if a prior failed run left stale baseline entries that corrupt the mutation_count==0 assertion.
findings.location: Signal definitions — 'Echo suppression: … verify mutation_count==0 on the next pass over just-written data (harness pre-seeds the baseline as a real pass would)'
findings.suggested_fix: Instantiate ProvenanceLedger with a test-run-specific storage path (e.g., `ledger_<run_id>.db`) created in setup and deleted in the bilateral teardown. Assert `ledger.mutation_count() == 0` immediately after instantiation (before seeding) to catch stale files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the C4 signal in a confirmed ACLI exit-code mapping: before writing the harness assertion, document (in the plan or the C4 ticket) whether the ACLI Go binary exits with code 429 on a Jira 429 response (vs. generic exit 1), and revise the 'simulated 429 exit' harness accordingly.
findings.finding: The C4 signal asserts 'assert the JITTERED-BACKOFF behavior C4 lands (a simulated 429 exit -> backoff -> success)' as if the ACLI Go subprocess reliably exits with code 429 on a Jira 429 response. This is an unverified capability of the ACLI binary: `_run_acli` currently catches `subprocess.CalledProcessError` (any non-zero exit) with fixed 2s/4s sleeps and no jitter; the 429-specific jittered logic exists only in `_call_with_backoff` (urllib HTTPError path). Whether ACLI maps HTTP 429 to exit code 429 (vs. exit 1) determines whether C4 can implement per-exit-code branching at all, and the plan's probe scope is deferred to the C4 ticket. The validation harness cannot assert 'jittered backoff behavior C4 lands' without first confirming this mapping.
findings.location: Signal definitions — C4 (429/backoff)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'recorded in an ADR/design note' with a specific observable outcome, e.g. 'a file `docs/adr/0042-e2e-harness.md` exists in the repo with sections: Context, Decision (harness pattern chosen), Consequences, and Teardown guarantee mechanism.'
findings.finding: The AC 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' is effort-framed and lacks a specific observable outcome — no location, no required content, no format — making it unmeasurable and indistinguishable from 'write something somewhere'.
findings.location: AC line 6: 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the artifact tracker (list/file) is initialized/opened BEFORE the first Jira create call, so any crash after creation is guaranteed to record the artifact id for teardown.
findings.finding: The plan requires tracking 'which of N synthetic artifacts were created' for partial-teardown safety, but does not specify that this tracking structure must be initialized BEFORE the first artifact-creation call. If the harness crashes (e.g., OOM, signal) after creating artifact #1 but before the tracker is populated, that artifact will not appear in leaked-artifacts.log and will survive undetected.
findings.location: Harness section — 'tracks WHICH of N synthetic artifacts were created (partial-setup)'
findings.suggested_fix: Use an append-to-file strategy: open `created-artifacts.log` for append before the first create, write each artifact id immediately after the create API call returns (not after all creates). The teardown reads this file, not an in-memory list, so it survives crashes.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
